bytes.topic: Architecture & Structure — file-based routing as architecture, route-level colocation of loader/error/loading
bytes.title: Astro File Routing as Architecture — Colocating Data, Errors, and Navigation State at the Route Level
bytes.body:
Astro's `src/pages/` directory isn't just routing — it's your architecture boundary. Each `.astro` file owns its full request lifecycle: the `---` frontmatter block is your loader, executing server-side before any markup renders. Unlike Next.js route segments, Astro colocates data fetching, redirects, and auth guards in a single file with no extra conventions to learn.

For dynamic routes, `getStaticPaths()` returns the param matrix at build time; flip `export const prerender = false` per-route for SSR without touching anything else. Error handling at the route level means returning `Astro.redirect('/404')` from the frontmatter block, or placing `404.astro` directly in `src/pages/` for unmatched paths — no special export required.

Colocate route-private components using the `_` underscore prefix: `src/pages/blog/_PostCard.astro` stays adjacent to its consumer but never becomes a URL. For client-navigation loading states, `<ViewTransitions />` in your layout head activates `transition:animate` directives per element, giving each route declarative enter/exit control without a separate loading file. The page IS the vertical slice.

bytes.topic: Component Design — Server Components vs Client Components, push use client to the leaves
bytes.title: Astro Islands — Push client: Directives to the Leaves, Not the Roots
bytes.body: Every Astro component is server-rendered by default — zero JS shipped. Hydration only begins when you attach a `client:*` directive to a framework component. The critical mistake is attaching that directive too high in the tree. If you slap `client:load` on a `<ProductPage />` React component because one nested button needs a click handler, you've hydrated the entire subtree: images, static headings, price text — all now part of a JS bundle. Astro's island architecture is designed to prevent exactly this. The fix: decompose. Keep `ProductPage.astro` as a static `.astro` file, pull the interactive piece into its own component — `<AddToCartButton client:load />` — and drop only that into the page. The rest renders at build time, ships no JS, and costs nothing at runtime. Use `client:visible` for below-the-fold islands to further defer hydration. The rule: an island's boundary should be the smallest interactive unit, never a layout container. Measure with `astro build && astro preview` — check page weight in DevTools before and after splitting.

bytes.topic: State Management — URL as state for filters/pagination, server state in loaders not useState
bytes.title: Astro State Management — URL and Loaders Beat useState for Filters and Pagination
bytes.body:
Astro pages are server-rendered by default, which means the right place for filter and pagination state is the URL — not a client-side signal or useState call. Use `Astro.url.searchParams` in your `.astro` component's frontmatter to read query params directly on the server, then pass the results as props to your islands. This keeps the page shareable, bookmarkable, and SSR-consistent without any client hydration cost.

For data fetching, Astro's frontmatter IS your loader. Fetch filtered data there using `searchParams.get('page')` or `searchParams.get('category')`, then render the result statically. If you're using Astro DB or an adapter like Drizzle, query inside frontmatter and pass typed props down — no `useEffect`, no loading spinners, no client state.

Reserve client-side state (via Nano Stores or framework signals) only for genuinely ephemeral UI state — open/close toggles, hover effects. Filters and pagination belong in the URL so Astro's server rendering can own the data layer entirely.

bytes.topic: Performance — SSG vs SSR vs ISR decision matrix, parallel data fetching in loaders
bytes.title: Astro Rendering Modes — SSG vs SSR vs ISR Decision Matrix and Parallel Frontmatter Fetching
bytes.body:
Astro's rendering modes map directly to data freshness requirements. Use SSG (`export const prerender = true`, the default) for documentation, blog posts, and marketing pages — data baked at build time, zero runtime cost, peak Lighthouse scores. Flip to SSR (`prerender = false` with `output: 'server'` in `astro.config.mjs`) for auth-gated routes, personalized dashboards, or any page that reads `Astro.locals` or `Astro.cookies`. For content that's mostly static but needs periodic refresh, pair a Vercel or Netlify adapter with `export const revalidate = 60` for ISR semantics without a full rebuild.

The critical mistake on SSR pages is sequential `await`s in frontmatter — each one suspends the entire render. Fan out with `Promise.all()` instead; Astro's frontmatter is async-first so parallel resolution is native. `getCollection()` and `getEntry()` are cached in-process, but every external API call must be parallelized manually.

Decision rule: if `Astro.locals` or `Astro.cookies` are touched → SSR. If data changes faster than your deploy cadence → ISR/revalidate. Otherwise → SSG.

bytes.topic: Data Fetching — route loaders fetch before render, eliminate waterfall, no fetch inside components
bytes.title: Astro Data Fetching — Route-Level Loads That Kill Waterfalls
bytes.body:
Astro's `getStaticProps`-equivalent lives inside `---` frontmatter for static pages, but for SSR routes (output: 'server'), data loading happens in the same frontmatter block — executed server-side before a single byte of HTML is streamed. This means zero client-side fetch waterfalls by design.

Inside `src/pages/posts/[slug].astro`, you call `Astro.params`, hit your DB or API, and pass data directly to the template — no useEffect, no loading spinner, no sequential fetches. For parallel data needs, use `Promise.all` in frontmatter: both queries fire simultaneously, and the page renders only after all data resolves.

Astro's middleware (`src/middleware.ts`) lets you attach data to `locals` before the page handler runs — useful for auth context or shared DB connections that every route needs. Access it via `Astro.locals` in any page.

Never reach for `fetch` inside an Astro component's template expression. That runs per-render without caching, defeats streaming, and reintroduces the waterfall. Keep all I/O in frontmatter or middleware — Astro enforces this naturally since component scripts don't have async lifecycle hooks.

bytes.topic: Forms — Server Actions, progressive enhancement, form validation on server boundary
bytes.title: Astro Actions — Forms That Work Without JavaScript
bytes.body: Astro Actions (stable since 4.9) wire server logic directly to HTML forms with Zod validation running at the server boundary before your handler is called. Define actions in `src/actions/index.ts` using `defineAction` with an `input` schema from `astro:schema` — invalid submissions never reach the handler; Astro returns a typed `ActionError` automatically. Set `accept: 'form'` to parse `multipart/form-data` or `application/x-www-form-urlencoded` natively. Bind the action via `action={actions.contact}` on any `<form method="POST">` — Astro serializes the RPC endpoint URL. Progressive enhancement is built-in: no JS means a full-page POST round-trip, add a client `enhance` hook for fetch-based submission without duplicating logic. Read results in the same page's frontmatter with `Astro.getActionResult(actions.contact)`, which returns `{ data, error }` after a POST and `undefined` on GET. Validation errors are fully typed — `result.error.fields.email` exposes Zod messages per field, no manual `try/catch` at the boundary required.

bytes.topic: TypeScript — typed route params, typed loaders, typed search params with Zod
bytes.title: Astro TypeScript — Typed Route Params, Content Schemas, and Zod Search Params
bytes.body:
Astro's type safety story is strongest when you combine `getStaticPaths`, `astro:content` schema inference, and Zod-validated search params.

Route params get their types automatically from `getStaticPaths` — Astro infers `Astro.params` to match exactly what you return. In `[slug].astro`, the moment `getStaticPaths` returns `{ params: { slug: post.slug } }`, `Astro.params.slug` is typed as `string` without casting.

For Content Layer schemas (Astro 5+), define your collection in `src/content.config.ts` via `defineCollection({ loader: glob(...), schema: z.object({...}) })`. Entries are then fully typed through `getCollection` and `getEntry` — no manual type assertions needed.

Search params are the weakest link — `Astro.url.searchParams` returns raw strings. Parse them with Zod at the top of the frontmatter block using `safeParse(Object.fromEntries(...))`. `z.coerce.number()` handles numeric query strings cleanly, and `.default()` eliminates optional chaining downstream.

This three-layer approach — inferred route types, schema-driven content types, Zod-parsed search params — gives you end-to-end type safety across every Astro page without a separate type file.

bytes.topic: Error Handling — error.tsx / +error.svelte per route segment, notFound(), redirect()
bytes.title: Astro Error Handling: Custom 404 Pages, SSR Response Guards, and Redirects
bytes.body:
Astro has no per-route error segments like Next.js's `error.tsx` or SvelteKit's `+error.svelte`. Error boundaries are global: place `src/pages/404.astro` and `src/pages/500.astro` in your pages directory — Astro's runtime renders them automatically when a matching HTTP status is returned.

For dynamic SSR routes, guard missing data by returning a `Response` directly from the frontmatter. `return new Response(null, { status: 404 })` triggers your `404.astro` — no helper function required. The same pattern works in `.ts`/`.js` API endpoints.

`Astro.redirect()` handles navigation cases — bouncing unauthenticated users, canonical slug migrations, or deprecated URLs. It defaults to `302`; use `301` for permanent moves that matter for SEO indexing.

Critical gotcha: `return new Response(...)` only works under `output: 'server'` or `output: 'hybrid'`. In fully static builds, Astro pre-renders a `404.html` at build time — you must configure your host (e.g., `staticwebapp.config.json` on Azure Static Web Apps, `_redirects` on Netlify) to serve it on unmatched routes.

bytes.topic: Testing — test loaders and actions in isolation, Playwright for full route flows
bytes.title: Astro Testing — Unit-Test Loaders and Actions, Then Validate Routes with Playwright
bytes.body:
Astro's Content Layer loaders and server Actions are pure async functions — unit-test them with Vitest before touching a browser. For a custom loader, mock the `LoaderContext`: stub `store.set`, pass a real `Map` as `meta`, supply a `parseData` spy, and call `loader.load()` directly. This lets you verify ETag caching logic, data normalization, and `generateDigest` behavior with zero network calls and fast feedback.

For Actions, call the exported handler directly in Vitest. Because `defineAction` wraps a typed, Zod-validated handler, you can pass raw objects and assert on the return shape — no HTTP layer, no form encoding, no overhead.

Playwright fills the gap Vitest can't: full route flows where SSR, middleware, and Actions interact end-to-end. In `playwright.config.ts`, add a `webServer` block pointing to `astro dev --host` on port 4321. Navigate to a route, submit a `<form>` wired to an Action (e.g., `action={actions.subscribe}`), and assert on the resulting DOM. This chain — Vitest for logic, Playwright for routes — keeps unit tests fast and E2E tests meaningful.

bytes.topic: Security — Server Actions as trust boundary, validate all input, env var separation
bytes.title: Astro Server Actions — Treating the Server Boundary as a Security Boundary
bytes.body:
Astro Server Actions (`src/actions/index.ts`) form a hard trust boundary — they execute exclusively on the server but are invocable from any `.astro` component or client island via `actions` from `astro:actions`. That boundary is only as strong as your validation.

Every action accepting client input must declare an `input` Zod schema. Astro validates against it automatically and surfaces a typed `ActionInputError` on failure — skip it and you're trusting raw POST bodies. The `handler` only receives parsed, safe input after the schema passes.

Env var separation is equally critical. Astro exposes `import.meta.env` to both client and server contexts. Any variable without the `PUBLIC_` prefix is stripped from client bundles at build time — but a typo (`PUBLISHABLE_KEY` instead of `PUBLIC_PUBLISHABLE_KEY`) silently ships secrets. Use Astro 5's `astro:env` module with `envField.string({ context: "server", access: "secret" })` to turn accidental client exposure into a build-time error, not a runtime leak.

Within `handler`, authenticate via `ctx.locals` (populated by your middleware) — never derive auth state from user-controlled request headers.

bytes.topic: Code Quality — route segment conventions, consistent loader/action naming
bytes.title: Astro Route Conventions — Segment Naming and Action Consistency at Scale
bytes.body:
Astro's file router silently enforces naming contracts. A `[slug].astro` page requires `getStaticPaths()` to return `params: { slug }` — a mismatch causes a cryptic build error. Use semantic segment names tied to content type: `[slug]` for human-readable identifiers, `[id]` for UUIDs, `[...path]` for nested catch-alls. Prefix non-route files with `_` (`_queries.ts`, `_utils.ts`) so Astro excludes them from routing without moving them out of `src/pages/`.

For API endpoints, always export named HTTP verbs (`GET`, `POST`) — Astro ignores default exports on `.ts` endpoint files. With Astro Actions (v4+), split handlers into namespaced files under `src/actions/` and compose them in `index.ts` through a single `server` export. Name every action with a verb-noun pair: `createPost`, `deleteComment`, `publishDraft`. This makes call sites like `actions.blog.createPost()` self-documenting across any component or page. Mixing imperative (`handle`, `submit`) and noun-only (`post`, `comment`) naming in the same actions layer is the fastest path to an unmaintainable server boundary.

bytes.topic: Routing — file-based routing patterns, dynamic segments, catch-all, intercepting routes, parallel routes
bytes.title: Astro Routing — Dynamic Segments, Catch-Alls, and What Doesn't Exist
bytes.body:
Astro's `src/pages/` directory drives routing. Static files map 1:1 to URLs. Dynamic segments use bracket syntax — `[slug].astro` captures a single segment via `Astro.params.slug`. For SSG, pair it with `getStaticPaths()` returning `{ params }` objects; for SSR, read params directly and return a 404 response manually with `new Response(null, { status: 404 })`.

Catch-all routes (`[...slug].astro`) match zero or more segments. Pass `{ params: { slug: undefined } }` in `getStaticPaths()` to cover the base path (e.g. `/docs`), and `'api/endpoints'` to cover `/docs/api/endpoints`. At runtime `Astro.params.slug` is a raw string — split on `/` to get segments. It is never an array.

Specificity wins in conflicts: `posts/[slug].astro` always beats `[...slug].astro` for `/posts/hello`.

Astro has no intercepting or parallel routes — those are Next.js App Router constructs. For parallel UI concerns, use named slots: `<slot name="sidebar" />` in a shared layout achieves the same compositional result without any routing magic.

bytes.topic: Internationalization — i18n routing strategies, locale in URL vs cookie, server-side locale detection
bytes.title: Astro i18n Routing — URL Prefixes, Cookies, and Server-Side Locale Detection
bytes.body:
Astro 4.x ships built-in i18n routing via the `i18n` config key. Set `defaultLocale` and `locales`, then pick a strategy: `"pathname"` (default) prefixes non-default locales in the URL (`/fr/blog`), while `"domains"` maps locales to full domain names.

For URL-based routing, `prefixDefaultLocale: true` forces `/en/` on the default locale too — critical for clean canonicals and avoiding duplicate-content penalties. Your `src/pages/` tree mirrors the URL: `src/pages/en/`, `src/pages/fr/`.

On the server side, `Astro.currentLocale` gives the active locale derived from the URL segment. For first-visit auto-redirect, `Astro.preferredLocale` parses the `Accept-Language` header and matches it against your configured locales. `Astro.preferredLocaleList` returns the full ranked list for granular fallback logic.

Cookie-based locale sits outside Astro's native i18n — handle it in `src/middleware.ts` using `context.cookies.get('locale')` before the route resolves. Priority chain: cookie (user's explicit choice) → `preferredLocale` (header-derived, first visit) → `defaultLocale`. This keeps UX consistent without client-side flicker.

bytes.topic: Web Vitals — image optimization with framework Image component, font loading, LCP via priority flag
bytes.title: Astro Web Vitals — Image Optimization, Font Loading, and LCP with astro:assets
bytes.body: Astro's `` component from `astro:assets` automatically converts images to WebP/AVIF, injects explicit `width`/`height` attributes to eliminate layout shift (CLS), and generates optimized srcsets — zero config. For LCP, the critical flag is `loading="eager"` combined with `fetchpriority="high"` on your above-the-fold hero image. Without this, browsers deprioritize the image during speculative parsing, killing your LCP score. For fonts, skip Google Fonts CDN entirely. Host font files locally under `public/fonts/`, then `` them in your layout's ``. Pair this with `font-display: swap` in CSS. Astro layouts give you direct `` control — no framework abstraction in the way. Use `` when you need art direction or want to ship AVIF with a WebP fallback. The `formats` prop controls output types, and `widths` + `sizes` drives responsive behavior. Avoid raw `` tags for any content images — you lose the sharp image service pipeline entirely, including the build-time dimension inference that prevents CLS.

bytes.topic: Memory Leaks — streaming with Suspense, abort signals in loaders, cleanup on navigation
bytes.title: Astro Memory Leaks — Abort Signals in SSR Streaming and View Transitions Cleanup
bytes.body:
Astro's SSR streaming sends HTML incrementally as async components resolve. If the client disconnects mid-stream, `Astro.request.signal` fires — but downstream `fetch()` calls inside `.astro` files or API routes won't cancel unless you explicitly forward that signal. In any SSR page, destructure and thread it through every fetch. In API routes, pull it from `APIContext`. If your endpoint spawns its own `AbortController`, chain it: listen for `Astro.request.signal` abort and call `controller.abort()` in that handler.

View Transitions introduces a second leak vector. With `<ViewTransitions />` active, client scripts from the outgoing page survive until the swap phase completes. Any `setInterval`, `EventSource`, or streaming fetch initialized in a `<script>` block keeps running on the next route. Cancel them in `astro:before-preparation`, which fires before Astro fetches the next page's HTML — earlier than `astro:before-swap`, giving pending requests time to terminate cleanly. Always pass `{ once: true }` to the listener; without it, the handler itself accumulates across navigations and fires multiple times per transition.

bytes.topic: Real-time & WebSockets — server-sent events from route handlers, combining with client state
bytes.title: Astro SSE — Streaming from API Routes and Syncing with Island State
bytes.body:
Astro API routes (`src/pages/api/*.ts`) can stream Server-Sent Events by returning a `Response` wrapping a `ReadableStream` with `Content-Type: text/event-stream`. Your adapter (Node, Vercel, Netlify) must support streaming — verify this before shipping.

On the client, native `EventSource` connects to the route. Inside Astro's `<script>` tag, you can dispatch custom events or manipulate the DOM directly. If you're using a React or Svelte island, instantiate `EventSource` inside `useEffect` / `onMount` — it's browser-only and will break SSR hydration if called at module scope.

The cleanest pattern for multi-island state: write incoming SSE payloads to a `nanostores` atom (`@nanostores/react` or `@nanostores/svelte`). Any island subscribing to that atom re-renders automatically without prop-drilling or lifting state into a parent layout.

Critical gotcha: if your project uses hybrid rendering with a static default, your SSE route must explicitly opt out with `export const prerender = false`. Without it, Astro pre-renders the route at build time and returns a hollow static response instead of a live stream.

bytes.topic: Environment & Config — NEXT_PUBLIC_ vs private vars, validate at startup, never expose secrets
bytes.title: Astro Environment Variables — PUBLIC_ Prefix, Private Secrets, and astro:env Validation
bytes.body:
Astro exposes variables to the client bundle only when prefixed with `PUBLIC_`. Anything without that prefix is server-only — never inlined into the client. This is Astro's equivalent of Next.js's `NEXT_PUBLIC_` convention, but enforced differently.

The modern way to handle this is `astro:env`, introduced in Astro 5. Define a schema in `astro.config.mjs` using `envField`. Astro validates every declared variable at startup — missing or maltyped secrets throw immediately, not at runtime when a user triggers the affected path.

Import server secrets from `astro:env/server` and public vars from `astro:env/client`. Attempting to import a `context: "server"` variable inside a `.astro` component's client-side `<script>` tag is a type error. The module boundary enforces what the prefix convention only suggests.

Never rely on `import.meta.env` directly for secrets in production code — it skips validation. Always go through the typed `astro:env` imports so TypeScript and the Astro build pipeline can catch exposure mistakes at compile time rather than in a breach postmortem.

bytes.topic: API Layer — route handlers for public APIs, Server Actions for mutations, middleware for auth
bytes.title: Astro API Layer — Route Handlers, Server Actions, and Auth Middleware Done Right
bytes.body: Astro gives you three distinct primitives for your API layer — each with a clear job. Route handlers in `src/pages/api/` export named HTTP verbs (`GET`, `POST`) and are ideal for public endpoints consumed by third parties or client-side `fetch`. They receive a typed `APIContext` with `request`, `params`, and `locals`. Server Actions (`defineAction` in `src/actions/index.ts`) are for mutations triggered from your own UI — they're type-safe, support Zod input validation out of the box, and are called with `actions.foo()` from any component. Middleware in `src/middleware.ts` uses `defineMiddleware` (or `sequence()` for composing multiple guards) to run before every request. Set `context.locals.user` here after verifying a Clerk or JWT session — both route handlers and Server Actions inherit `locals` from the same `APIContext`, so auth logic stays in one place. Never duplicate auth checks inside individual actions; push the boundary to middleware and trust `locals` downstream.

bytes.topic: Animation — page transition patterns, View Transitions API, layout persistence across navigations
bytes.title: Astro View Transitions — Page Animations and Layout Persistence Without a SPA
bytes.body:
Astro's View Transitions API ships as a first-class component via `astro:transitions`. Drop `<ViewTransitions />` into your layout's `<head>` and Astro converts every MPA navigation into an animated, SPA-like page swap — zero client JS bundle overhead required.

Shared-element morphs use `transition:name`. Give the same name to a matching element on both pages and the browser interpolates position, size, and opacity automatically. Astro bundles four `transition:animate` presets — `fade`, `slide`, `morph`, and `none` — all overridable with a custom `AnimationBuilder` or raw CSS `@keyframes`.

For components that must survive navigation intact — audio players, scroll position, form state — `transition:persist` prevents DOM teardown. The node is physically moved into the incoming page rather than destroyed and remounted, preserving in-memory React/Svelte/Vue state.

Programmatic navigation goes through `navigate()` from `astro:transitions/client`. For side effects, listen to `document` lifecycle events: `astro:before-preparation` (intercept), `astro:after-preparation` (modify DOM before swap), and `astro:after-swap` (reinitialize third-party widgets). Browsers without View Transitions support fall back to normal navigation — no polyfill needed.
bytes.code_snippet: --- // Layout.astro import { ViewTransitions } from 'astro:transitions'; const { post } = Astro.props; ---

bytes.topic: Global UI Patterns — layout-level toast/modal, shared loading UI in layout segment
bytes.title: Astro Global UI — Layout-Level Toasts & Modals with transition:persist
bytes.body:
Astro's layout system paired with View Transitions and `transition:persist` is the right primitive for global UI shells. Place interactive islands — Toast, Modal, PageLoader — directly in your base layout and annotate them with `transition:persist`. Astro will skip tearing down and remounting those islands between client-side navigations, preserving component state across route changes.

For shared state, reach for `nanostores`. Define a `$toast` atom in a shared module; any island on any page can write to it without prop drilling or React context. The Toast island reads `$toast` reactively and renders independently of the page content beneath it.

Loading UI follows the same pattern: a `<PageLoader />` island with `transition:persist` listens to `astro:before-preparation` (navigation start) and `astro:page-load` (navigation complete) from the View Transitions event lifecycle to drive a progress indicator. No router wrapper needed — Astro dispatches these events on `document` automatically.

This is cleaner than framework-level providers: the Astro layout *is* the shell, `transition:persist` makes it sticky, and nanostores handle cross-island communication.
bytes.code_snippet:
---
// src/layouts/Base.astro
import { ViewTransitions } from 'astro:transitions';
import Toast from '@/islands/Toast';
import PageLoader from '@/islands/PageLoader';
---
<html lang="en">
  <head><ViewTransitions /></head>
  <body>
    <PageLoader client:load transition:persist="page-loader" />
    <Toast client:load transition:persist="global-toast" />
    <slot />
  </body>
</html>

bytes.topic: Bundle & Asset Optimization — automatic code splitting per route, dynamic imports, bundle analysis
bytes.title: Astro Bundle Optimization — Code Splitting, Dynamic Imports, and Bundle Analysis
bytes.body:
Astro ships zero JavaScript by default, and its Islands Architecture ensures each `client:*` directive generates an isolated chunk scoped to that component alone — not the whole page. Route-level splitting is automatic: `dist/_astro/` contains per-route JS bundles loaded only on the matching page, with no manual configuration required.

For heavy components, use dynamic imports inside `.astro` frontmatter to defer parsing until runtime:

```astro
const { default: Chart } = await import('../components/Chart.tsx');
```

Pair this with `client:visible` to delay hydration until the element enters the viewport, cutting both network transfer and parse cost on initial load.

To audit what ships, add `rollup-plugin-visualizer` to your Vite config — Astro's build pipeline is Vite under the hood. After `astro build`, open `dist/stats.html` for a treemap of every chunk.

Use `manualChunks` in `vite.build.rollupOptions` to pin heavy shared deps like `recharts` or `three` into named vendor chunks. Without this, Astro may duplicate them across multiple island bundles that share the same dependency, bloating total transfer size.

bytes.topic: Dev Tooling & Quality Gates — framework CLI, preview deployments, type-check route params
bytes.title: Astro Route Params Are Fully Typeable — Here's the Right Way
bytes.body: Astro's CLI ships three quality gates most teams underuse: `astro check`, `astro build`, and `astro preview`. Run them in sequence to catch issues before they hit a CDN. `astro check` runs the TypeScript compiler across all `.astro` files — including template expressions that `tsc` alone misses. For dynamic routes, Astro infers param types directly from `getStaticPaths` via `InferGetStaticParamsType` and `InferGetStaticPropsType`. Accessing `Astro.params.slug` without this inference types it as `string | undefined`, masking bugs. With proper inference, the type narrows to exactly the keys you defined. Wire `astro check` into CI before `astro build`, then run `astro preview` against the built `dist/` — it spins up the SSR adapter locally, so edge-case 404s and redirect loops surface before the deploy. For monorepos, pass `--root ./apps/web` to scope the check. This three-command pipeline (`check → build → preview`) eliminates an entire class of runtime errors that only appear post-build.

bytes.topic: Monitoring in Production — edge function logs, server-side Sentry, Web Vitals from RUM
bytes.title: Astro Production Monitoring — Edge Logs, Sentry SSR, and Web Vitals from RUM
bytes.body:
Astro's `@sentry/astro` integration auto-instruments SSR routes and API endpoints — add it via `integrations: [sentry({ dsn, tracesSampleRate })]` in `astro.config.mjs` and source maps upload on every build automatically. No manual `Sentry.init()` needed in server code.

For edge function logs, define `src/middleware.ts` using `defineMiddleware` from `astro:middleware`. The middleware runs per-request at the edge (Vercel/Cloudflare adapters), giving you structured timing and request ID propagation via `ctx.locals`. Attach `ctx.locals.requestId` and call `Sentry.setTag` inside the same scope to correlate edge logs with Sentry traces end-to-end.

Web Vitals need a client island. Import `onCLS`, `onLCP`, `onINP` from `web-vitals` inside a `client:load` component and flush to your RUM endpoint. If `<ViewTransitions />` is enabled, re-register on `document.addEventListener('astro:page-load', registerVitals)` — without this, SPA-style navigations silently skip LCP re-sampling. Use `astro:after-swap` if you need to reset state before the new page renders.

bytes.topic: Middleware — run at the edge before render, auth guards, A/B testing, geolocation, rate limiting
bytes.title: Astro Middleware — Edge Guards, A/B Tests, and Geo Routing Before the Page Renders
bytes.body:
Astro middleware lives in `src/middleware.ts` and runs on every request before any page or endpoint renders — or at the edge if your adapter supports it (Vercel, Cloudflare). Use `defineMiddleware` to intercept `context` and `next`. Attach anything to `context.locals` and it flows directly into `Astro.locals` inside `.astro` files and API routes — no prop drilling.

For auth guards, read a session cookie via `context.cookies.get()` and call `context.redirect('/login')` before `next()`. For A/B testing, assign a variant to a cookie on first visit and store it in `locals.variant` so the page renders the right branch server-side — no layout shift. For geolocation, Cloudflare injects `cf-ipcountry` headers you can read off `context.request.headers` to serve region-locked content. Rate limiting works the same way: check a KV store or Upstash Redis in middleware, return a `new Response('Too Many Requests', { status: 429 })` directly — bypassing `next()` entirely. Chain independent concerns with Astro's `sequence()` helper to keep each middleware focused and composable.
